query: BaseRT: 6.4x faster than llama.cpp, 3.9x faster than MLX alternative

results.title: BaseRT Review - AI Tool Features and Tips
results.content:
Blazing Speed: Up to 6.4x faster than llama.cpp and 3.9x faster than MLX on prefill, and significant gains on decode.
 One-Command Installation: Get up and running instantly with `curl -LsSf  | sh`.
 Offline Operation & Privacy: Run models entirely on your device, ensuring no data leaves your machine and no API keys are needed.
 Coding Agent Integration: Seamlessly serve models for local coding agents, keeping your entire development workflow private and on-device.
 Broad Model Support: From Qwen to Llama, Gemma, Mistral, Phi-3, and Nomic BERT, a wide array of popular open-source models are supported.

## Who’s BaseRT For? Empowering On-Device AI Engineers [...] Visit Website  Have an AI Tool? Submit it here!

The benchmarks speak for themselves. BaseRT isn’t just a marginal improvement; it’s a paradigm shift in local LLM execution. It achieves staggering speedups against industry standards, turning your Apple Silicon machine into a formidable AI powerhouse.

|  |  |
 --- |
| Benchmark (Tokens/sec) | BaseRT vs. Competitors |
| Prefill: Qwen3 30B-A3B (pp128) | 2,478 (+94% vs MLX, 6.4x vs llama.cpp) |
| Decode: Qwen3 0.6B (tg128Q4) | 531 (+33% vs MLX, +38% vs llama.cpp) |
| Prefill: Gemma 4 E2B (pp2048) | 16,264 (+32% vs MLX, 6.4x vs llama.cpp) |

## Core Capabilities: Unleashing Local LLMs [...] AI Tool Archive Logo

1. Home
2. AI Productivity

# BaseRT Review

Last modified: |  Fred Magni

Dive into the future of local AI with BaseRT, the definitive performance champion for Large Language Models on Apple Silicon. Forget cloud dependencies and API costs; BaseRT empowers you to run advanced LLMs directly on your device, delivering unparalleled speed and complete data privacy. It’s purpose-built to obliterate performance bottlenecks, making your local AI experience dramatically faster.

results.title: LLM订阅服务，高速模型，零Token费
results.content:
### 应用场景

 智能客服与语音助手：低延迟实时响应，适用于语音代理和交互式 AI 产品
 代码生成与编程辅助：高吞吐量支持编码代理、批量任务和并行生成，提升开发效率
 企业知识库问答：262K 超长上下文窗口支持长文档处理，缓存机制大幅降低多轮对话成本
 敏感业务负载：专属端点隔离共享池，满足金融、医疗、政务等行业的合规与数据安全要求

### 目标受众

面向需要高性能、低成本、易集成大模型推理服务的中大型企业、AI 应用开发团队及技术决策者，特别适合对延迟敏感、有合规要求或追求极致性价比的组织。

results.title: BaseRT: Best-in-Class LLM Inference on Apple Silicon via ...
results.content: Table 1 reports decode throughput (tg128) on the M4 Pro. Against llama.cpp, BaseRT is faster on all six models, with speedups of 1.041.04–1.56×1.56\times. The advantage is largest on the smaller dense models, where fixed per-token overhead is a greater fraction of latency: 56%56\% improvement on Qwen3-0.6B. It narrows on the larger mixture-of-experts models, namely to 1.041.04–1.07×1.07\times on the Qwen3-30B-A3B and Gemma-4-26B-A4B MoE models, as decode becomes increasingly memory-bandwidth-bound. Against MLX, BaseRT leads on four of the five models supported by MLX (1.011.01–1.35×1.35\times); the exception is Gemma-4-26B-A4B, where MLX is faster (0.90×0.90\times ratio). Similar to the trend with parameter count, the relative advantage of BaseRT is larger at Q4 than at Q8, as the smaller [...] Gemma 4 families at Q4 and Q8 quantisation on M3 and M4 Pro devices. BaseRT achieves up to 1.56×1.56\times higher decode throughput than llama.cpp and up to 1.35×1.35\times higher than MLX, with substantially larger margins on prefill for mixture-of-experts models, delivering consistent best-in-class throughput from sub-1B to 30B parameter models. These results establish Apple Silicon as a more capable inference platform than previously reported, with direct implications for the emerging edge inference paradigm: as privacy requirements, latency constraints, and cloud cost pressures drive inference toward on-device deployment, performance-optimised local runtimes are a critical enabling layer for this transition. BaseRT is publicly available at . [...] The dominant open-source runtimes for Apple Silicon each carry architectural constraints that limit how fully they exploit this topology. The llama.cpp  framework was originally developed as a cross-platform, CPU-first codebase and subsequently extended with a Metal backend. Its architecture reflects these origins. MLX , while purpose-built for Apple Silicon, is a general-purpose array framework whose lazy evaluation and framework-level scheduling introduce overhead not present in a dedicated inference runtime [9, 3]. Approaches building on top of MLX including vllm-mlx have demonstrated strong results within these constraints, reporting 21-87% higher throughput than llama.cpp .

results.title: Benchmarking Apple's MLX vs. llama.cpp | by Andreas Kunar
results.content:
My results for llama-2–7B fp16:

   llama.cpp: model-load ~2.8s, prompt-processing ~772 token/s, token-generation ~23 token/s, ~16 GB RAM used (pure model: 12.55 GB)
   MLX: model-load ~4s, prompt-processing ~652 token/s, token-generation ~19 token/s, ~ 16 GB RAM used

My results for llama-2–7B 4-Bit quantized:

   llama.cpp: model-load ~0.9s, prompt-processing ~685 token/s, token-generation ~61 token/s, ~6 GB RAM used (pure model: 3.56 GB)
   MLX: model-load ~1.7s, prompt-processing ~438 token/s, token-generation ~31 token/s, ~ 6 GB RAM used [...] _Note:_ prompt-processing token/s results vary a lot with prompt-length and batch-size, detailed comparisons are complicated. I used the same prompt-length and token-generation length as llama.cpp in their benchmark results for all Apple silicon here. The artificially large 512-token prompt is in order to test the GPU limits, token-generation is mostly memory-bandwidth constrained.

_I hope this provides you with food for thought. MLX’s performance really surprised me, I would have thought, that MLX would be much slower._

_P.S.: Updated this article on Dec 26 with entirely new benchmarking numbers, in order to better compare it to the llama.cpp Apple silicon performance results._

   llama.cpp with Llama-2–7B in fp16 and Q4_0 quantization. Download and generate the fp16 GGUF-file from the huggingface repository. For the Q4_0 quantization, TheBloke’s provides GGUF files (but please observe the llama-2 license!).
   mlx-examples and installation instructions in it’s llm/llama folder. You need to either convert the Llama-2–7B weights from the above step’s huggingface files, or download according to the README.md. Using llama.py to test the models
   I tweaked llama.py to llama_bench.py for printing the timings in a manner comparable to llama.cpp. This is a quick&dirty hack to get some results, not professional coding. Just copy the llama_bench file to your llm/llama folder above and run it there.
